generatedBy: @miaggy/mcp-audit — run `npm run build:catalog` to regenerate from src/rules/catalog.ts
note: Single source of truth is src/rules (RuleSpec.threat/rationale + TOOL_FINDING_METADATA). This file is a generated artifact; do not edit by hand.

rules.title: MCP server runs an unpinned npm package
rules.threat: A hijacked maintainer account or a malicious patch release becomes arbitrary code execution inside the agent host on the next client start, with no action by the user.
rules.rationale: Pinning turns a silent auto-upgrade channel into an explicit review point. This is the same guidance this project ships for its own install instructions.

rules.title: Credential stored inline in MCP config
rules.threat: Client config files are world-readable to local processes, sync to backups and dotfile repos, and get pasted into bug reports. A literal credential in one is a theft waiting for an occasion.
rules.rationale: Environment indirection ("${VAR}") or OS keychains keep the secret out of a file that routinely travels. The finding names the variable, never the value.

rules.title: Server package has no registry provenance
rules.threat: Without provenance, a tampered or account-takeover release is indistinguishable from a legitimate one at install time; trust rests entirely on whoever controls the npm credentials.
rules.rationale: Provenance moves trust from a stealable token to a verifiable build. Once present, 'npm audit signatures' catches substitution mechanically.

rules.title: Server package declares install scripts
rules.threat: Install hooks run arbitrary code at install time, before the server is ever started: the classic npm supply-chain execution primitive.
rules.rationale: MCP servers rarely need native builds. An install hook in a tool-server package deserves a manual look before the package is trusted.

rules.title: Server package shows low maintenance signals
rules.threat: Single-maintainer, long-stale packages are the easiest takeover targets (expired maintainer emails, unpatched dependencies) and the slowest to ship a fix when one is needed.
rules.rationale: Maintenance signals are weak evidence individually, so this is cataloged low: it informs review without failing a CI gate.

rules.title: MCP config file could not be read
rules.threat: An unparseable config silently hides servers from the audit; the user believes coverage is complete when part of it is dark.
rules.rationale: The honest output for a broken discovery path is a skip finding, never a silent empty. Same pattern as the bedrock pack's detection tool.

rules.title: npm registry lookup skipped
rules.threat: Missing registry data silently disables three rules; a clean report would overstate what was checked.
rules.rationale: Surfacing the skipped lookup keeps the report honest about coverage instead of implying the registry-backed checks passed.
